library(rvest)
library(dplyr)
library(corrplot)
library(purrr)

page <- read_html("https://www.tennis-warehouse.com/reviewedracquets.html")

models <- page %>% html_nodes("div.cattable-wrap-cell.gtm_impression") %>% html_attr("data-gtm_impression_name")
brand <- page %>% html_nodes("div.cattable-wrap-cell.gtm_impression") %>% html_attr("data-gtm_impression_brand")
code <- page %>% html_nodes("div.cattable-wrap-cell.gtm_impression") %>% html_attr("data-gtm_impression_code")

links <- page %>% html_nodes("div.cattable-wrap-cell-imgwrap.is-racquet") %>% html_nodes("a") %>% html_attr("href")
links <- paste0("https://www.tennis-warehouse.com", links)

additional <- c("https://www.tennis-warehouse.com/learning_center/racquet_reviews/PRGOSreview.html",
                "https://www.tennis-warehouse.com/learning_center/racquet_reviews/97V13Rreview.html",
                "https://www.tennis-warehouse.com/learning_center/racquet_reviews/PS97RFreview.html",
                "https://www.tennis-warehouse.com/learning_center/racquet_reviews/P97CVreview.html",
                "https://www.tennis-warehouse.com/learning_center/racquet_reviews/wblxbtreview.html",
                "https://www.tennis-warehouse.com/learning_center/racquet_reviews/hyigroreview.html")

links <- c(links, additional)
models <- c(models, c("Prince Original Graphite 107", "Wilson Pro Staff RF97 v13", "Wilson Pro Staff RF97 Autograph",
                      "Wilson Pro Staff 97 Countervail", "Wilson BLX Blade Tour", "Head Youtek IG Radical OS"))
code <- c(code, "PRGO", "97V13", "PS97R", "PS97CV", "WBLXBT", "HYIGRO")
brand <- c(brand, c("Prince", "Wilson", "Wilson", "Wilson", "Wilson", "Head"))


process_page <- function(link, model, brand, code) {
  snames <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("div.scores tr th") %>% html_text()
  svals <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("div.scores tr td") %>% html_text()
  if (length(snames) == 0) {
    sall <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("div.review_scores tr td") %>% html_text()

    if (length(sall) != 0) {
      snames <- sall[seq(1,length(sall), by=2)]
      svals <- sall[seq(2,length(sall), by=2)]
    }
  }

  if (length(snames) == 0) {
    sall <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("div.scores table tr td") %>% html_text()

    if (length(sall) != 0) {
      snames <- sall[seq(1,length(sall), by=2)]
      svals <- sall[seq(2,length(sall), by=2)]
    }

    if (length(sall) == 0) {
      sall <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("div.score_box tr td") %>% html_text()

      if (length(sall) != 0) {
        snames <- sall[seq(1,length(sall), by=2)]
        svals <- sall[seq(2,length(sall), by=2)]
      }
    }
  }


  rdc_ratings <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("table.racquet_rate_table") %>% html_nodes("td") %>% html_text2(preserve_nbsp = TRUE)
  if (length(rdc_ratings) == 0) {
    rdc_ratings <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("table td table") %>% html_nodes("font") %>% html_text2(preserve_nbsp = TRUE)
    ind1 <- grep("Flex Rating", rdc_ratings)
    ind2 <- grep("Swing Weight", rdc_ratings)
    rdc_ratings <- rdc_ratings[c(ind1+1, ind2+1)]
  } else {
    tval <- as.numeric(rdc_ratings[2])
    if (!is.na(tval) && tval < 80 & tval > 40) {
      rdc_ratings <- rdc_ratings[c(2,4)]
    } else {
      ind1 <- grep("Flex Rating", rdc_ratings)
      ind2 <- grep("Swing Weight", rdc_ratings)
      rdc_ratings <- rdc_ratings[c(ind1+1, ind2+1)]
    }
  }

  print(rdc_ratings)

  specs <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("table.racquet_specs_table") %>% html_nodes("tr td") %>% html_text()
  if (length(specs) == 0) {
     specs <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("#main_content > center > table > tr > td:nth-child(1)") %>% html_text2(preserve_nbsp = TRUE)
     specs<- try(stringr::str_split(specs, "[\t\n]")[[1]])

     if (inherits(specs, "try-error")) {
       specs <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("center > table > tr > td:nth-child(1)") %>% html_text2(preserve_nbsp = TRUE)
       specs<- try(stringr::str_split(specs, "[\t\n]")[[1]])
      }
  }

  indiv <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes("div.individ_scores") %>% html_nodes("div.tablewrap") %>% html_nodes("table")
  if (length(indiv) == 0) {
    indiv <- read_html(link) %>% html_nodes(".tablewrap")  %>% html_nodes("table")
  }

  indiv <- lapply(indiv, function(x) {
    sname <- x %>% html_nodes("tr th") %>% html_text()
    sname <- iconv(sname, from = 'UTF-8', to = 'ASCII//TRANSLIT')
    sname <- gsub("Chris' Scores", "Chris", sname)
    sname <- gsub("Tiffani' Scores", "Tiffani", sname)
    sname <- gsub("James' Scores", "James", sname)
    sname <- gsub("Z'sofia", "Zsofi", sname)
    sname <- gsub("'$", "", sname)
    sname <- stringr::str_trim(gsub("'s Scores", "", sname))
    sval <- x %>% html_nodes("tr td") %>% html_text()

    category <- c("Power", "Serves", "Control", "Groundstrokes", "Maneuverability", "Returns",
               "Stability", "Slice", "Comfort", "Topspin", "Touch/Feel", "Volleys")
    scores <- sapply(category, function(cat) {
               gind <- grep(cat, sval)
               if (length(gind) > 0) {
                 sval[gind+1]
               }
    })


    tibble(player=sname,category=category, score=scores)
  }) %>% dplyr::bind_rows()

  specnames <- c("Length", "Head Size", "Weight", "Balance Point", "Construction", "Composition", "String Pattern")

  if (length(specs) == 10) {
    spind <- grep("/", specs)
    construction <- grep("mm", specs)[1]
    specvals <- specs[c(1,3,5,7, construction, construction+1,spind[length(spind)])]
  } else if (length(specs) == 11) {
    construction <- grep("mm", specs)[1]
    spind <- grep("/", specs)
    specvals <- specs[c(1,3,5,7,construction,construction+1, spind[length(spind)])]
  }else {
    #browser()
    specvals <- lapply(specnames, function(sp) {
    gind <- grep(sp, specs)
    if (length(gind) > 0) {
      specs[gind+1]
    } else {
      NA
    }
    })
  }

  print(specvals)

  indiv %>% mutate(model=model, code=code, brand=brand, flex=rdc_ratings[1], swingweight=rdc_ratings[2],
                   length=specvals[[1]], head_size=specvals[[2]], weight=specvals[[3]],
                   balance=specvals[[4]], construction=specvals[[5]], material=specvals[[6]],
                   string_pattern=specvals[[7]])
}

ret <- lapply(1:length(links), function(i) {
  print(paste(i, ":", links[i]))
  process_page(links[i], models[i], brand[i], code[i])
})

ret <- do.call(rbind, ret)

ret <- ret %>% mutate(thickness={
  tmp <- stringr::str_split(stringr::str_replace_all(construction, "mm", ""), "/")
  sapply(tmp, function(x) mean(as.numeric(x), na.rm=TRUE))
}) %>% mutate(mains = {
  tmp <- stringr::str_split(string_pattern, "/")
  unlist(lapply(tmp, function(x) {
    vals <- as.numeric(stringr::str_replace(x, "([a-zA-Z].*)", ""))
    as.numeric(vals[[1]])
  }))
}) %>% mutate(crosses = {
  tmp <- stringr::str_split(string_pattern, "/")
  unlist(lapply(tmp, function(x) {
    vals <- as.numeric(stringr::str_replace(x, "([a-zA-Z].*)", ""))
    as.numeric(vals[[2]])
  }))
}) %>% mutate(balance_inches = {
  bp2 = str_split(balance, "in")
  bp2 = lapply(bp2, "[[", 1)
  as.numeric(bp2)
})


mnest = ret %>% nest_by(model)
player_ret <- lapply(1:nrow(mnest), function(i) {
  dat <- mnest$data[[i]]
  mod <- mnest$model[i]
  scores <- split(dat, dat$player) %>% map(function(x) as.numeric(x$score))
  players <- names(scores)

  smat <- do.call(rbind, scores)
  smat <- as.matrix(smat)
  smat[smat > 10] <- smat[smat > 10]/10

  avg <- colMeans(smat, na.rm=TRUE)
  delta <- apply(smat, 1, function(vals) sum((vals-avg)^2))
  cor <- apply(smat, 1, function(vals) cor(vals,avg, method="spearman"))
  global <- rowMeans(smat)
  data.frame(player=players, delta=delta, cor=cor, global=global, model=mod)
}) %>% bind_rows()

player_ret_sum <- player_ret %>% group_by(player) %>% summarize(global=mean(global),
                                                                cor=mean(cor), delta=mean(delta),
                                                                count=n()) %>% arrange(desc(cor)) %>%
                                                                mutate(weightfac=(scale(.5*rank(cor))[,1] + .5*scale(1/rank(delta))[,1] +
                                                                       2*scale(rank(count))[,1])/3
                                                                ) %>% arrange(desc(weightfac))

ret <- inner_join(ret, player_ret_sum)

avg_scores <- ret %>% nest_by(model)
avg <- lapply(1:nrow(avg_scores), function(i) {
  print(i)
  dat <- avg_scores$data[[i]]
  mod <- avg_scores$model[i]
  scores <- split(dat, dat$player) %>% map(function(x) as.numeric(x$score))
  wts <- as.numeric(split(dat, dat$player) %>% map(function(x) as.numeric(x$weightfac[1])))
  wts <- pnorm(wts)
  players <- names(scores)

  smat <- do.call(rbind, scores)
  smat <- as.matrix(smat)

  smat[smat > 10] <- smat[smat > 10]/10


  avg <- apply(smat,2, function(vals) weighted.mean(vals, wts))
  data.frame(model=mod, avgscore=avg, category=avg_scores$data[[1]]$category[1:12])
}) %>% bind_rows()

avg <- left_join(ret, avg, by=c("model", "category"))
finalavg <- avg %>% group_by(model, category) %>% summarize(score=mean(avgscore),
                                                            code=code[1],
                                                            brand=brand[1],
                                                            flex=flex[1],
                                                            swingweight=swingweight[1],
                                                            length=length[1],
                                                            head_size=head_size[1],
                                                            weight=weight[1],
                                                            balance=balance[1],
                                                            balance_inches=balance_inches[1],
                                                            thickness=thickness[1],
                                                            mains=mains[1],
                                                            cross=crosses[1],
                                                            material=material[1],
                                                            string_pattern=string_pattern[1])

sessions <- finalavg %>% ungroup() %>% nest_by(model)
sessmat <- sessions$data %>% map(function(dat) {
  sc <- dat$score
  names(sc) <- dat$category
  sc
}) %>% bind_rows()

vnames <- colnames(sessmat)
sessmat <- apply(sessmat, 2, function(x) (x-min(x))/(max(x) - min(x)))
csessmat <- t(apply(sessmat, 1, function(x) scale(x, center=TRUE, scale=FALSE)))

sessmat2 <- sessions$data %>% map(function(dat) {
  head_size=as.numeric(stringr::str_trim(gsub("[a-z]", "", dat$head_size[1])))
  length=as.numeric(stringr::str_trim(gsub("[a-z]", "", dat$length[1])))

  tmp = data.frame(weight=as.numeric(stringr::str_trim(gsub("[a-z]", "", dat$weight[1]))),
             flex=as.numeric(dat$flex[1]),
             length=length[1],
             thickness=dat$thickness[1],
             head_size=head_size[1],
             swingweight=as.numeric(dat$swingweight[1]),
             string_density=((dat$mains/head_size + dat$cross/head_size)/2)[1],
             balance_pts=(length[1] - dat$balance_inches[1])/(1/8))


}) %>% bind_rows()

sessmat2 <- apply(sessmat2, 2, function(x) {
  z = which(is.na(x))
  x[z] <- mean(x,na.rm=TRUE)
  x
})

modnames <- unique(finalavg$model)
row.names(sessmat) <- modnames
colnames(sessmat) <- vnames
colnames(csessmat) <- vnames

library(glmnet)

X <- as.data.frame(scale(sessmat2))
Y <- as.data.frame(scale(sessmat))
Yc <- as.data.frame(scale(csessmat))

glm.1 <- glmnet(X, Y, family="mgaussian", lambda=.2)
glm.2 <- glmnet(X, Yc, family="mgaussian", lambda=.2)

cv1 <- cv.glmnet(as.matrix(X), as.matrix(Y), alpha=.5, family="mgaussian")
cv2 <- cv.glmnet(as.matrix(X), as.matrix(Yc), alpha=.5, family="mgaussian")

preds <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:nrow(Yc), function(i) {
  Xt <- X[-i,]
  Yt <- Yc[-i,]

  Xtest <- X[i,]
  Ytest <- Yc[i,]

  glm.1 <- glmnet(Xt, Yt, family="mgaussian", alpha=.5, lambda=.2)
  p <- predict(glm.1, as.matrix(Xtest))

}))

library(explor)
library(FactoMineR)
library(ade4)

pres <- PCA(scale(sessmat))
explor(pres)


#sessmat <- t(apply(sessmat,1, function(x) scale(x, center=TRUE, scale=TRUE)))

bothmat <- cbind(csessmat, sessmat2)
row.names(bothmat) <- modnames
pres2 <- PCA(scale(bothmat))
explor(pres2)

library(GPArotation)
library(psych)
#fres <- factanal(bothmat, factors=3, rotation="varimax")
fres <- fa(bothmat, nfactors=3, rotate="varimax")
pc1 <- prcomp(bothmat)
pc1$sdev <- c(1,1,1)
pc1$rotation <- as.matrix(loadings(fres))
class(pc1$rotation) <- "matrix"
colnames(pc1$rotation) <- paste0("PC", 1:3)
pc1$x <- scale(bothmat) %*% pc1$rotation
colnames(pc1$x) <- paste0("PC", 1:3)
explor(pc1)

# app.R
library(shiny)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(DT)
library(plotly)
library(ggplot2)
library(stringr)
library(ggrepel)

# Source module files
source("modules/mod_review_table.R")
source("modules/mod_pca_ui.R")
source("modules/mod_pca_server.R")
source("modules/mod_cancor_ui.R")
source("modules/mod_cancor_server.R")


#-----------------------
# 1) Load raw data
#-----------------------
all_review_data <- read.table("all_review_data.txt", header = TRUE, sep = "", quote = "\"",
                              stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)

# 1) Read data
all_review_data <- read.table("all_review_data.txt", header = TRUE, sep = "",
                              quote = "\"", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)

# 1) Read your data
all_review_data <- read.table("all_review_data.txt",
                              header = TRUE, sep = "", quote = "\"",
                              stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# 2) Clean up data
all_review_data_clean <- all_review_data %>%
  # Remove rows with missing/empty category
  filter(!is.na(category), category != "") %>%
  # Remove "Final Verdict" if not wanted
  filter(category != "Final Verdict") %>%
  # Merge alternate "Touch/Feel"
  mutate(category = case_when(
    category %in% c("Touch / Feel", "Touch/Feel") ~ "TouchFeel",
    TRUE ~ category
  )) %>%
  # Trim whitespace
  mutate(category = str_trim(category)) %>%
  # Filter out leftover empty categories
  filter(category != "")

# 3) (Optional) Rename original columns if desired
all_review_data_clean <- all_review_data_clean %>%
  rename(
    # Keep original text columns
    weight_original    = weight,
    length_original    = length,
    head_size_original = head_size,
    balance_original   = balance
  ) %>%
  # Rename the numeric columns (assuming numeric versions were created earlier)
  rename(
    weight     = weight_numeric,
    length     = length_numeric,
    head_size  = head_size_numeric
    # We keep "balance_diff_in" as-is
  )

# 4) Define specification columns (excluding "player")
spec_cols <- c("brand", "model", "code", "flex", "swingweight", "length",
               "head_size", "weight", "balance_diff_in", "construction",
               "material", "string_pattern")

# 5) Average scores across playtesters for each code and category
averaged_data <- all_review_data_clean %>%
  group_by(across(all_of(c(spec_cols, "category")))) %>%
  summarize(score = mean(score, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# 6) Pivot to wide form
pivoted_df <- averaged_data %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = all_of(spec_cols),
    names_from = "category",
    values_from = "score"
  )

# Now 'pivoted_df' contains 'balance_diff_in' as your main numeric measure of headlightness,
# plus the other numeric columns (length, head_size, weight) in tidy form, suitable for PCA.


#-----------------------
# 4) Define UI
#-----------------------
ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Tennis Warehouse Racquet Reviews"),
  tabsetPanel(
    tabPanel("Data Table", mod_review_table_ui("table1")),
    tabPanel("PCA Plot", mod_pca_ui("pca1")),
    tabPanel("CCA Analysis", mod_cancor_ui("cca1"))
  )
)

#-----------------------
# 5) Define server
#-----------------------
server <- function(input, output, session) {

  # Call the table module
  mod_review_table_server("table1", full_data = pivoted_df)

  # Call the PCA module
  mod_pca_server("pca1", data_pivoted = pivoted_df)

  mod_cancor_server("cca1", data_pivoted = pivoted_df)
}

# Run the app
shinyApp(ui, server)

# mod_pca_ui.R
mod_pca_ui <- function(id, label = "PCA") {
  ns <- shiny::NS(id)

  tagList(
    fluidRow(
      column(
        width = 3,
        wellPanel(
          h4("PCA Options"),
          helpText("Pick a numeric variable to color the PCA points by:"),
          uiOutput(ns("color_var_ui")),
          uiOutput(ns("vars_for_arrows_ui")),
          sliderInput(
            ns("arrow_text_size"),
            "Arrow label text size:",
            min   = 1,
            max   = 10,
            value = 3
          ),
          checkboxInput(
            ns("normalize_rows"),
            "Normalize Subjective Ratings by Row",
            value = FALSE
          )
        )
      ),
      column(
        width = 9,
        plotly::plotlyOutput(ns("pca_plot"), height = "600px"),
        div(style = "margin-top: 10px; font-size: 16px; font-weight: bold;",
            textOutput(ns("hover_model")))
      )
    )
  )
}

input.plan:
## Tennis Warehouse Racquet Analysis - Comprehensive Codebase Review

Based on my thorough examination of the codebase, here's what I found:

### **Project Overview**
This is an R-based data science project that **scrapes, analyzes, and visualizes tennis racquet reviews** from Tennis Warehouse. The project combines web scraping, statistical analysis, and interactive Shiny applications.

### **Core Components Found**

#### **1. Web Scraping Infrastructure**
- **Main scraping engine**: `main.R` (1,126 lines) - comprehensive scraping framework
- **Discovery tool**: `discover_racquets.R` (787 lines) - finds racquet review pages
- **Legacy scraper**: `scrape_reviews.R` (331 lines) - older scraping approach
- **Data source**: `racquets.csv` - 104 racquet models from brands like Babolat, Wilson, Head, Yonex, etc.

#### **2. Data Processing Pipeline**
- Scrapes Tennis Warehouse review pages for:
  - Player ratings across 12 categories (Power, Control, Serves, etc.)
  - Technical specifications (weight, length, head size, flex, swingweight)
  - Individual tester scores vs. staff averages
- **Output**: `all_review_data.txt` - processed review data with 15 columns
- **Processed data**: `racquet_datamat.rds` - analysis-ready dataset

#### **3. Interactive Applications**
- **Main app**: `racquet_app/` - modular Shiny application with:
  - Data table viewer
  - PCA visualization with interactive plots
  - Canonical correlation analysis (CCA)
- **Simple explorer**: `racquet_space/` - basic PCA exploration tool
- **Deployment**: RSConnect configuration for shinyapps.io

#### **4. Statistical Analysis Features**
- **Weighted scoring system**: Accounts for reviewer reliability/consistency
- **Dimensionality reduction**: PCA and factor analysis
- **Predictive modeling**: GLMNET for racquet characteristic prediction
- **Correlation analysis**: Cross-category relationships

### **Key Data Insights**
- **104 racquet models** across 8+ major brands
- **12 performance categories** rated by multiple testers
- **Technical specifications** including RDC machine measurements
- **Reviewer weighting** based on consistency and correlation with group averages

### **Technical Stack**
- **R packages**: rvest, dplyr, shiny, plotly, FactoMineR, glmnet, corrplot
- **Web scraping**: Handles both old and new Tennis Warehouse page layouts
- **Data visualization**: Interactive PCA plots with hover details
- **Deployment**: Shiny apps deployable to shinyapps.io

### **Code Quality Assessment**
- **Well-structured**: Modular design with clear separation of concerns
- **Robust scraping**: Multiple fallback strategies for different page layouts
- **Comprehensive**: Covers full pipeline from scraping to visualization
- **Documentation**: Functions include detailed comments and parameters
- **No security concerns**: Legitimate web scraping for research purposes

This is a sophisticated tennis equipment analysis system that would be valuable for players, coaches, and equipment researchers.

## Modern Tennis Racquet Explorer - Design Vision

Based on your rich dataset, I envision a sleek, modern web application that transforms tennis racquet selection from guesswork into an intuitive, data-driven experience.

### **Core Concept: "Racquet DNA Explorer"**

**Tagline**: *"Discover the perfect racquet through the intersection of feel and physics"*

### **Key Features & User Experience**

#### **1. Interactive Racquet Space Visualization**
- **3D scatter plot** showing racquets positioned by their characteristics
- **Hover interactions** reveal detailed specs and ratings
- **Dynamic filtering** by brand, price range, player level
- **Similarity clustering** - find racquets like your current one

#### **2. Characteristic Correlation Engine**
- **Visual correlations** between physical specs and subjective ratings
- "If you like **powerful groundstrokes**, you'll want **higher swingweight**"
- **Smart recommendations** based on playing style preferences

#### **3. Racquet Comparison Tool**
- **Side-by-side comparisons** with radar charts
- **Difference highlighting** - what makes each racquet unique
- **Performance predictions** based on your preferences

#### **4. Discovery Tools**
- **Style-based finder**: "I'm an aggressive baseliner" → recommendations
- **Upgrade path**: Show progression from beginner to advanced racquets
- **Similar players**: "Players like you also chose..."

### **Technical Architecture**

- `GET /correlations` - Spec-to-performance relationships

### **Implementation Strategy**

2. **Comparison Tool**: Side-by-side analysis
3. **Performance Animations**: Smooth transitions
4. **Analytics**: User behavior tracking

#### **Technology Choices**
- **Why Next.js**: Server-side rendering, great performance, modern React
- **Why D3.js**: Best-in-class data visualization library
- **Why PostgreSQL**: Complex queries, JSON support, reliability
- **Why TypeScript**: Type safety, better developer experience

### **Unique Value Propositions**

1. **"Physics to Feel" Translation**: Show users exactly how weight, balance, and string pattern translate to power, control, and comfort
2. **Smart Discovery**: "Show me racquets that are more powerful but just as comfortable"  
3. **Visual Clustering**: See racquets grouped by playing style rather than just brand
4. **Evidence-Based**: Every recommendation backed by real player data, not marketing

### **Example User Journeys**

**Beginner Player**:
"I'm new to tennis" → System suggests lighter, more forgiving racquets → Shows progression path to intermediate models

**Upgrading Player**: 
"I currently play with a Babolat Pure Drive" → Shows similar options → Highlights key differences → Suggests optimal upgrades

**Advanced Player**:
Adjusts precise specs (swingweight, flex) → System updates recommendations in real-time → Shows performance trade-offs

This would transform racquet selection from overwhelming to intuitive, creating a beautiful, educational, and genuinely useful tool for tennis players of all levels.

# Modern Tennis Racquet Explorer - Implementation Plan

## Project Overview
Transform the existing R-based tennis racquet analysis into a modern, elegant web application that helps users explore the relationship between physical racquet characteristics and performance ratings.

## Technical Architecture

### Frontend Stack
- **Next.js 14** with TypeScript for modern React development
- **Tailwind CSS** for responsive, utility-first styling  
- **D3.js** for interactive data visualizations
- **Three.js** for 3D racquet space exploration
- **Framer Motion** for smooth animations

### Backend Stack  
- **PostgreSQL** database with optimized queries
- **Node.js API** (or Python FastAPI) for data endpoints
- **Data migration** from existing R analysis to JSON/API format

### Key Features to Build

#### 1. Interactive Racquet Space (Primary Feature)
- 3D scatter plot positioning racquets by PCA coordinates
- Hover interactions showing detailed specs and ratings
- Dynamic filtering by brand, weight, head size, playing style
- Smooth zoom/pan controls with physics-based animations

#### 2. Smart Recommendation Engine
- "Find racquets similar to..." functionality
- Style-based recommendations ("aggressive baseliner" → racquet suggestions)
- Performance correlation insights ("more power usually means less control")

#### 3. Comparison & Analysis Tools
- Side-by-side racquet comparisons with radar charts
- Performance prediction based on physical specs
- Visual correlation between technical specs and subjective ratings

#### 4. Modern UX Design
- Minimalist tennis court aesthetic (green/white color scheme)
- Mobile-first responsive design
- Intuitive navigation and progressive disclosure
- Fast, app-like performance

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1 (Weeks 1-2): Foundation
1. Set up Next.js project with TypeScript and Tailwind
2. Create PostgreSQL database and migrate racquet data
3. Build basic API endpoints for racquet data
4. Implement simple 2D visualization prototype

### Phase 2 (Weeks 3-4): Core Features
1. Develop 3D interactive racquet space with D3.js/Three.js
2. Add filtering and search functionality
3. Create detailed racquet profile pages
4. Implement responsive design for mobile

### Phase 3 (Weeks 5-6): Advanced Features  
1. Build recommendation engine using similarity algorithms
2. Add racquet comparison tool with radar charts
3. Implement smooth animations and micro-interactions
4. Add user analytics and performance optimization

## Data Processing Pipeline
- Convert R analysis outputs to PostgreSQL tables
- Precompute PCA coordinates for fast rendering
- Create search indexes for efficient filtering
- Generate similarity matrices for recommendations

## Success Metrics
- Fast load times (<3s initial, <1s interactions)
- Intuitive user experience (minimal learning curve)
- Accurate recommendations (validated against existing R analysis)
- Mobile-friendly responsive design
- Modern, tennis-themed aesthetic

This will create a sophisticated, user-friendly tool that makes tennis racquet selection data-driven and intuitive.

# Tennis Racquet Explorer - 4-Sprint Development Plan

## Overview
Build a modern, elegant web application for exploring tennis racquets, transforming the existing R analysis into an interactive, beautiful user experience. Each sprint includes development work followed by comprehensive code audits using Gemini Pro and O3 models.

## Sprint Structure & Methodology

### Development Process
1. **Sprint Development** (5-7 days): Core feature implementation
2. **Code Audit Phase** (2-3 days): 
   - Automated analysis with Gemini Pro (architecture, performance, best practices)
   - Deep review with O3 (logic, edge cases, optimization opportunities)
3. **Feedback Integration** (1-2 days): Address major concerns before next sprint
4. **Sprint Review**: Validate deliverables and plan next iteration

## Sprint Breakdown

### Sprint 1: Foundation & Core Architecture
**Goal**: Solid technical foundation with basic data visualization

**Deliverables**:
- Next.js 14 + TypeScript project setup
- PostgreSQL database with migrated racquet data
- Basic REST API for racquet data
- Simple 2D scatter plot visualization
- Core UI framework with tennis-themed design system

### Sprint 2: Interactive Data Exploration
**Goal**: Rich, interactive visualization with filtering capabilities

**Deliverables**:
- 3D interactive racquet space with D3.js/Three.js
- Dynamic filtering system (brand, specs, performance)
- Racquet detail modal/pages
- Responsive mobile design
- Search functionality

### Sprint 3: Intelligence & Comparison Features
**Goal**: Smart features that provide real value to users

**Deliverables**:
- Recommendation engine (similarity-based suggestions)
- Side-by-side racquet comparison tool
- Performance correlation insights
- Advanced filtering (playing style, upgrade paths)
- User preference learning

### Sprint 4: Polish & Production
**Goal**: Production-ready, beautiful, performant application

**Deliverables**:
- Performance optimization (lazy loading, caching)
- Advanced animations and micro-interactions
- Accessibility compliance (WCAG 2.1)
- SEO optimization
- Production deployment setup
- User analytics integration

## Quality Assurance Process

### Code Audit Framework
After each sprint:

1. **Gemini Pro Analysis**:
   - Architecture review and best practices
   - Performance bottleneck identification
   - Security vulnerability assessment
   - Code quality and maintainability

2. **O3 Deep Review**:
   - Logic flow analysis and edge case handling
   - Algorithm optimization opportunities
   - User experience flow validation
   - Integration testing recommendations

3. **Feedback Integration**:
   - Prioritize critical issues (security, performance)
   - Address architectural concerns
   - Implement suggested optimizations
   - Document decisions and trade-offs

### Success Criteria
- **Performance**: <3s initial load, <1s interactions
- **Quality**: 90+ Lighthouse score, zero critical issues
- **UX**: Intuitive navigation, mobile-friendly
- **Code**: TypeScript strict mode, 80%+ test coverage

## Technical Stack Decisions
- **Frontend**: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS
- **Visualization**: D3.js, Three.js for 3D interactions
- **Backend**: Node.js API or Python FastAPI
- **Database**: PostgreSQL with optimized queries
- **Deployment**: Vercel (frontend) + Railway/Fly.io (API)

This methodology ensures we build a robust, beautiful, and user-friendly tennis racquet exploration tool with proper quality gates at each stage.

## Development Framework

### Sprint-Based Development (4 Sprints Total)
- **Sprint Duration**: 7-10 days each
- **Sprint Goal**: Incremental feature delivery with quality gates
- **Final Delivery**: Production-ready, beautiful tennis racquet exploration app

### Quality Assurance Process

   - Architecture patterns and best practices
   - Performance bottleneck identification
   - Security vulnerability assessment
   - Code maintainability and documentation
   - TypeScript/JavaScript quality standards

2. **O3 Deep Review** (Logic & Optimization)
   - Algorithm efficiency and correctness
   - Edge case handling and error scenarios
   - User experience flow validation
   - Integration testing gaps
   - Optimization opportunities

3. **Feedback Integration Process**
   - **Critical Issues**: Security, performance blockers (must fix immediately)
   - **Major Concerns**: Architecture improvements, significant UX issues (address before next sprint)
   - **Minor Issues**: Code style, documentation (address as time permits)
   - **Enhancement Suggestions**: Log for future consideration

#### Sprint Review Checklist
- [ ] All sprint deliverables completed
- [ ] Code audit completed with both AI models
- [ ] Critical and major issues resolved
- [ ] Code committed with clear sprint tag
- [ ] Sprint retrospective documented
- [ ] Next sprint planning updated based on findings

### Technical Standards

#### Code Quality Requirements
- **TypeScript**: Strict mode enabled, no `any` types
- **Testing**: 80%+ code coverage, unit tests for all core functions
- **Performance**: Lighthouse score 90+, <3s initial load, <1s interactions
- **Accessibility**: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance
- **Security**: No critical vulnerabilities, secure API practices

#### Architecture Principles
- **Modern Stack**: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS
- **Responsive Design**: Mobile-first approach
- **Performance First**: Code splitting, lazy loading, optimized assets
- **Data-Driven**: Leverage existing R analysis for recommendations
- **User-Centric**: Intuitive tennis player experience

### File Organization
```
/tennis-racquet-explorer/
├── CLAUDE.md                 # This methodology document
├── sprint1.md               # Foundation & Core Architecture
├── sprint2.md               # Interactive Data Exploration  
├── sprint3.md               # Intelligence & Comparison Features
├── sprint4.md               # Polish & Production
├── docs/                    # Sprint reviews and decisions
│   ├── sprint1-review.md
│   ├── sprint2-review.md
│   ├── sprint3-review.md
│   └── sprint4-review.md
└── src/                     # Application code
```

### Development Commands
- `npm run dev` - Start development server
- `npm run build` - Production build
- `npm run test` - Run test suite
- `npm run lint` - Code quality check
- `npm run audit` - Security vulnerability scan

### AI Model Usage Guidelines

#### When to Use Gemini Pro
- Architecture and design pattern reviews
- Performance optimization suggestions
- Security best practices validation
- Code style and maintainability assessment

#### When to Use O3
- Complex algorithm logic verification
- Edge case identification and handling
- User flow optimization
- Deep technical problem solving

#### Code Review Prompts
```
For Gemini Pro:
"Review this [component/feature] for architecture, performance, and best practices. Focus on [specific concern]. Provide specific, actionable feedback."

For O3:
"Analyze this [algorithm/logic] for correctness and optimization. Consider edge cases and potential issues. Suggest improvements with reasoning."
```

### Success Metrics
- **User Experience**: Intuitive racquet discovery and comparison
- **Performance**: Fast, responsive interactions
- **Visual Design**: Clean, tennis-themed aesthetic
- **Data Accuracy**: Faithful to original R analysis insights
- **Code Quality**: Maintainable, well-tested, documented

### Sprint Communication
- Daily progress updates in sprint markdown files
- Issue tracking with clear severity levels
- Decision documentation with rationale
- Regular sprint goal validation

This methodology ensures we build a high-quality, beautiful, and functional tennis racquet explorer while maintaining code excellence through systematic AI-assisted reviews.

input.content:
# Sprint 1: Foundation & Core Architecture

**Duration**: 7-10 days  
**Goal**: Establish solid technical foundation with basic data visualization and modern tennis-themed design

## Sprint Objectives

### Primary Deliverables
1. **Modern Web Application Setup**
   - Next.js 14 with TypeScript configuration
   - Tailwind CSS for responsive styling
   - ESLint, Prettier, and strict TypeScript setup
   - Development environment with hot reload

2. **Data Infrastructure**
   - PostgreSQL database setup and schema design
   - Data migration from R analysis to PostgreSQL
   - REST API endpoints for racquet data
   - Data validation and type safety

3. **Basic Visualization**
   - Simple 2D scatter plot of racquets using D3.js
   - Interactive hover states showing racquet details
   - Basic filtering by brand and performance metrics
   - Responsive layout that works on mobile

4. **Design System Foundation**
   - Tennis-themed color palette and typography
   - Reusable UI components (buttons, cards, inputs)
   - Consistent spacing and visual hierarchy
   - Accessibility basics (focus states, contrast)

## Technical Architecture

### Frontend Stack
```
Next.js 14 (App Router)
├── TypeScript (strict mode)
├── Tailwind CSS (custom tennis theme)
├── D3.js (data visualization)
├── Radix UI (accessible components)
└── Framer Motion (animations)
```

### Backend & Data
```
PostgreSQL Database
├── racquets table (specs + performance data)
├── reviews table (individual tester scores)
├── brands table (manufacturer info)
└── Prisma ORM (type-safe database access)

API Routes (Next.js)
├── /api/racquets (GET with filtering)
├── /api/racquets/[id] (GET single racquet)
├── /api/brands (GET all brands)
└── /api/health (system status)
```

## Detailed Tasks

### 1. Project Setup (Days 1-2)
- [ ] Initialize Next.js 14 project with TypeScript
- [ ] Configure Tailwind CSS with custom tennis theme
- [ ] Set up ESLint, Prettier, and TypeScript strict rules
- [ ] Create folder structure following Next.js App Router patterns
- [ ] Set up development environment and scripts

### 2. Database Design & Migration (Days 2-3)
- [ ] Design PostgreSQL schema for racquet data
- [ ] Create Prisma schema and migration files
- [ ] Write R-to-PostgreSQL migration script
- [ ] Migrate all racquet data preserving relationships
- [ ] Create database indexes for performance

### 3. API Development (Days 3-4)
- [ ] Implement GET /api/racquets with filtering
- [ ] Implement GET /api/racquets/[id] for single racquet
- [ ] Add request validation using Zod schemas
- [ ] Implement error handling and logging
- [ ] Write API tests for core endpoints

### 4. Basic UI Components (Days 4-5)
- [ ] Create design system tokens (colors, fonts, spacing)
- [ ] Build reusable components (Button, Card, Select, Input)
- [ ] Implement responsive layout structure
- [ ] Add loading states and error boundaries
- [ ] Ensure accessibility (ARIA labels, keyboard navigation)

### 5. Initial Data Visualization (Days 5-7)
- [ ] Create 2D scatter plot component with D3.js
- [ ] Map racquet physical specs to X/Y coordinates
- [ ] Add interactive hover states with racquet details
- [ ] Implement basic filtering (brand, weight range)
- [ ] Make visualization responsive for mobile devices

### 6. Tennis-Themed Design (Days 6-7)
- [ ] Design tennis court inspired color palette
- [ ] Create logo and branding elements
- [ ] Implement smooth animations for interactions
- [ ] Add tennis ball loading spinner
- [ ] Polish visual hierarchy and typography

## Data Schema Design

### Primary Tables
```sql
-- Racquets table (main entity)
CREATE TABLE racquets (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  code VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  model VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  brand VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  
  -- Physical specifications
  weight_oz DECIMAL(4,2),
  length_in DECIMAL(4,2),
  head_size_sq_in INTEGER,
  balance_in DECIMAL(4,2),
  flex_rating INTEGER,
  swingweight INTEGER,
  string_pattern VARCHAR(10),
  construction VARCHAR(100),
  material VARCHAR(100),
  
  -- Performance averages (computed from reviews)
  power_avg DECIMAL(3,1),
  control_avg DECIMAL(3,1),
  comfort_avg DECIMAL(3,1),
  maneuverability_avg DECIMAL(3,1),
  stability_avg DECIMAL(3,1),
  
  -- Metadata
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Individual reviews/ratings
CREATE TABLE reviews (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  racquet_id INTEGER REFERENCES racquets(id),
  player_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  category VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
  score DECIMAL(3,1) NOT NULL,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

## UI/UX Wireframes

### Main Layout
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  🎾 Tennis Racquet Explorer          [Search] │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                 │
│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────────┐│
│  │   Filters   │  │     2D Scatter Plot       ││
│  │             │  │                           ││
│  │ Brand: All▼ │  │    ●  Wilson              ││
│  │ Weight:     │  │ ●     ● Babolat           ││
│  │ [290-320g]  │  │    ●                      ││
│  │             │  │        ● Head             ││
│  │ Performance:│  │                           ││
│  │ Power: [6-9]│  │  X: Weight → Y: Control   ││
│  │ Control:[7-9│  │                           ││
│  └─────────────┘  └─────────────────────────────┘│
│                                                 │
│  Selected Racquet Details                      │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ Wilson Pro Staff 97 v14                    │ │
│  │ 11.4 oz • 315 swingweight • 16x19          │ │
│  │ Power ████████░░ 8.2  Control ██████████ 9.1│ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Testing Strategy
- **Unit Tests**: All API endpoints and utility functions
- **Integration Tests**: Database operations and data migrations
- **Component Tests**: UI components with user interactions
- **E2E Tests**: Core user flows (filter, select, view details)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Application loads in under 3 seconds
- [ ] All racquet data successfully migrated from R analysis
- [ ] Interactive scatter plot with 100+ racquets displayed
- [ ] Filtering works smoothly with real-time updates
- [ ] Mobile responsive design works on phones/tablets
- [ ] Clean, tennis-themed visual design
- [ ] Zero TypeScript errors, 90+ ESLint score
- [ ] All API endpoints return correct data format

## Code Review Focus Areas
- **Architecture**: Next.js best practices, clean component structure
- **Performance**: Database query optimization, client-side rendering efficiency
- **Type Safety**: Comprehensive TypeScript coverage, API contracts
- **Accessibility**: Keyboard navigation, screen reader compatibility
- **Design**: Visual consistency, responsive behavior

## Sprint Review Checklist
- [ ] All primary deliverables completed
- [ ] Code committed with proper documentation
- [ ] Gemini Pro architecture review completed
- [ ] O3 logic and optimization review completed
- [ ] Critical issues identified and resolved
- [ ] Sprint retrospective documented
- [ ] Sprint 2 planning updated based on findings

---

**Next Sprint Preview**: Sprint 2 will focus on building the 3D interactive racquet space with advanced filtering and detailed racquet profile pages.

# Sprint 2: Interactive Data Exploration

**Duration**: 7-10 days  
**Goal**: Create rich, interactive 3D racquet visualization with advanced filtering and detailed racquet exploration

## Sprint Objectives

### Primary Deliverables
1. **3D Interactive Racquet Space**
   - Three.js powered 3D scatter plot visualization
   - Smooth camera controls (pan, zoom, rotate)
   - PCA-based positioning of racquets in 3D space
   - Interactive point selection with visual feedback

2. **Advanced Filtering System**
   - Multi-dimensional filtering (specs + performance)
   - Real-time filter updates with smooth animations
   - Saved filter presets ("Power Players", "Control Seekers")
   - Visual filter indicators showing active constraints

3. **Detailed Racquet Profiles**
   - Rich detail modal/pages for individual racquets
   - Performance radar charts with category breakdowns
   - Specification comparison with category averages
   - High-quality racquet images (if available)

4. **Enhanced Mobile Experience**
   - Touch-optimized 3D navigation
   - Collapsible filter panels for small screens
   - Gesture support (pinch to zoom, swipe to rotate)
   - Progressive disclosure of information

## Technical Implementation

### 3D Visualization Architecture
```
Three.js Scene
├── Camera Controls (OrbitControls)
├── Racquet Points (InstancedMesh for performance)
├── Axis Labels & Grid
├── Selection Highlighting
└── Smooth Animations (GSAP/Framer Motion)

Data Processing
├── PCA Coordinate Calculation
├── Real-time Filtering Logic
├── Spatial Clustering Algorithm
└── Performance-based Coloring
```

### Advanced State Management
```
Zustand Store
├── racquets/ (all racquet data)
├── filters/ (active filter state)
├── ui/ (selected racquet, modal state)
├── visualization/ (3D camera position, view mode)
└── preferences/ (user settings, saved filters)
```

## Detailed Tasks

### 1. 3D Visualization Foundation (Days 1-3)
- [ ] Set up Three.js scene with responsive canvas
- [ ] Implement camera controls with smooth animations
- [ ] Create racquet point geometry and materials
- [ ] Add axis labels and grid for orientation
- [ ] Implement point selection with ray casting

### 2. PCA Integration & Positioning (Days 2-3)
- [ ] Calculate PCA coordinates from racquet specifications
- [ ] Map 3D positions based on principal components
- [ ] Add color coding by performance categories
- [ ] Implement dynamic point sizing based on popularity
- [ ] Create smooth transitions between view modes

### 3. Advanced Filtering Interface (Days 3-5)
- [ ] Design multi-range slider components
- [ ] Implement category-based filtering (power, control, etc.)
- [ ] Add brand selection with visual brand indicators
- [ ] Create playing style presets ("Aggressive Baseliner")
- [ ] Build filter reset and clear functionality

### 4. Detailed Racquet Profiles (Days 4-6)
- [ ] Design racquet detail modal with smooth animations
- [ ] Implement performance radar chart with D3.js
- [ ] Add specification breakdown with visual comparisons
- [ ] Include player reviews and ratings summary
- [ ] Create "Find Similar" functionality

### 5. Mobile Optimization (Days 5-7)
- [ ] Implement touch controls for 3D navigation
- [ ] Design collapsible filter panels
- [ ] Add gesture recognition (pinch, swipe)
- [ ] Optimize performance for mobile devices
- [ ] Test on various screen sizes and orientations

### 6. Search & Discovery (Days 6-7)
- [ ] Implement intelligent search across racquet names/specs
- [ ] Add autocomplete with highlighting
- [ ] Create "Recently Viewed" racquet history
- [ ] Implement URL state management for sharing
- [ ] Add keyboard shortcuts for power users

## Enhanced UI Components

### 3D Visualization Controls
```tsx
// 3D Scene Controls Component
interface SceneControlsProps {
  onViewModeChange: (mode: 'pca' | 'specs' | 'performance') => void;
  onCameraReset: () => void;
  activeMode: string;
}

// Filter Panel with Real-time Updates
interface FilterPanelProps {
  filters: FilterState;
  onFilterChange: (filters: Partial<FilterState>) => void;
  presets: FilterPreset[];
  racquetCount: number;
}
```

### Performance Radar Chart
```tsx
// Radar Chart for Racquet Performance
interface RadarChartProps {
  data: {
    power: number;
    control: number;
    comfort: number;
    maneuverability: number;
    stability: number;
    touch: number;
  };
  comparison?: RadarChartProps['data']; // Compare with category average
  size?: 'small' | 'medium' | 'large';
}
```

## Data Processing Enhancements

### PCA Calculation Pipeline
```typescript
// PCA coordinate calculation from R analysis
interface PCACoordinates {
  x: number; // PC1 - Power vs Control axis
  y: number; // PC2 - Weight vs Maneuverability axis  
  z: number; // PC3 - Comfort vs Stability axis
}

// Real-time filtering with performance optimization
interface FilterProcessor {
  applyFilters(racquets: Racquet[], filters: FilterState): Racquet[];
  getFilteredCount(filters: FilterState): number;
  getSuggestedFilters(current: FilterState): FilterSuggestion[];
}
```

### Advanced Filtering Logic
```typescript
interface FilterState {
  // Physical specifications
  weight: [min: number, max: number];
  headSize: [min: number, max: number];
  swingweight: [min: number, max: number];
  
  // Performance characteristics
  power: [min: number, max: number];
  control: [min: number, max: number];
  comfort: [min: number, max: number];
  
  // Categorical filters
  brands: string[];
  stringPatterns: string[];
  playingStyles: PlayingStyle[];
  
  // Advanced options
  priceRange?: [min: number, max: number];
  availability?: 'in-stock' | 'all';
}
```

## Mobile-First Design Patterns

### Responsive 3D Controls
```
Desktop (>1024px):
├── Full filter panel (left sidebar)
├── 3D visualization (center)
├── Detail panel (right sidebar)
└── Traditional mouse controls

Tablet (768-1024px):
├── Collapsible filter overlay
├── Full-width 3D visualization
├── Bottom sheet for details
└── Touch-optimized controls

Mobile (<768px):
├── Hamburger menu for filters
├── Full-screen 3D visualization
├── Modal dialogs for details
└── Gesture-based navigation
```

## Performance Optimizations

### 3D Rendering Efficiency
- [ ] Use InstancedMesh for thousands of racquet points
- [ ] Implement level-of-detail (LOD) based on zoom
- [ ] Add frustum culling for off-screen points
- [ ] Use worker threads for PCA calculations
- [ ] Implement smart caching for filter results

### Data Loading Strategy
- [ ] Lazy load detailed racquet data
- [ ] Implement virtual scrolling for large lists
- [ ] Use React.memo and useMemo strategically
- [ ] Add service worker for offline capability
- [ ] Optimize bundle size with code splitting

## User Experience Flows

### Primary Discovery Flow
```
1. User lands on 3D racquet space
2. Sees all racquets positioned by similarity
3. Uses filters to narrow down options
4. Hovers/clicks to see quick details
5. Clicks "View Details" for full profile
6. Uses "Find Similar" to explore alternatives
```

### Advanced User Flow
```
1. User searches for specific racquet
2. Selects racquet to view details
3. Compares with 2-3 other options
4. Uses "Upgrade Path" to see progression
5. Saves preferred racquets to wishlist
6. Shares filtered view with coach/friend
```

## Testing & Quality Assurance

### Performance Testing
- [ ] 3D rendering performance on various devices
- [ ] Filter response time with large datasets
- [ ] Mobile touch responsiveness
- [ ] Memory usage optimization
- [ ] Battery impact on mobile devices

### User Experience Testing
- [ ] 3D navigation intuitiveness
- [ ] Filter discoverability
- [ ] Mobile gesture recognition
- [ ] Accessibility with keyboard navigation
- [ ] Cross-browser compatibility

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Smooth 60fps 3D visualization on desktop/mobile
- [ ] Sub-200ms filter response time
- [ ] Intuitive 3D navigation for first-time users
- [ ] All racquets properly positioned in 3D space
- [ ] Mobile touch controls work flawlessly
- [ ] Detailed racquet profiles load instantly
- [ ] Zero console errors or TypeScript issues
- [ ] Accessible to keyboard and screen reader users

## Code Review Focus Areas
- **3D Performance**: Three.js optimization, rendering efficiency
- **State Management**: Complex filter state handling
- **Mobile UX**: Touch interactions, responsive design
- **Data Processing**: PCA calculations, filtering algorithms
- **Accessibility**: 3D visualization accessibility patterns

## Sprint Review Checklist
- [ ] All primary deliverables completed and tested
- [ ] 3D visualization performs well on mobile devices
- [ ] Advanced filtering works with real-time updates
- [ ] Gemini Pro performance and architecture review
- [ ] O3 algorithm optimization and UX flow review
- [ ] Critical performance issues resolved
- [ ] Mobile experience validated on real devices
- [ ] Sprint retrospective with user feedback

---

**Duration**: 7-10 days  
**Goal**: Create rich, interactive 3D racquet visualization with advanced filtering and detailed racquet exploration

## Sprint Objectives

### Primary Deliverables
1. **3D Interactive Racquet Space**
   - Three.js powered 3D scatter plot visualization
   - Smooth camera controls (pan, zoom, rotate)
   - PCA-based positioning of racquets in 3D space
   - Interactive point selection with visual feedback

2. **Advanced Filtering System**
   - Multi-dimensional filtering (specs + performance)
   - Real-time filter updates with smooth animations
   - Saved filter presets ("Power Players", "Control Seekers")
   - Visual filter indicators showing active constraints

3. **Detailed Racquet Profiles**
   - Rich detail modal/pages for individual racquets
   - Performance radar charts with category breakdowns
   - Specification comparison with category averages
   - High-quality racquet images (if available)

4. **Enhanced Mobile Experience**
   - Touch-optimized 3D navigation
   - Collapsible filter panels for small screens
   - Gesture support (pinch to zoom, swipe to rotate)
   - Progressive disclosure of information

### 3D Visualization Architecture
```
Three.js Scene
├── Camera Controls (OrbitControls)
├── Racquet Points (InstancedMesh for performance)
├── Axis Labels & Grid
├── Selection Highlighting
└── Smooth Animations (GSAP/Framer Motion)

Data Processing
├── PCA Coordinate Calculation
├── Real-time Filtering Logic
├── Spatial Clustering Algorithm
└── Performance-based Coloring
```

### Advanced State Management
```
Zustand Store
├── racquets/ (all racquet data)
├── filters/ (active filter state)
├── ui/ (selected racquet, modal state)
├── visualization/ (3D camera position, view mode)
└── preferences/ (user settings, saved filters)
```

content.text:
Perfect! I've created a comprehensive 4-sprint development plan for transforming your tennis racquet analysis into a modern, elegant web application. Here's what we now have:

## 📋 Complete Sprint Plan Created

### **CLAUDE.md** - Development Methodology
- Comprehensive quality assurance process with AI code reviews
- Sprint-based development with systematic feedback integration
- Technical standards and architecture principles
- Success metrics and communication protocols

### **Sprint 1** - Foundation & Core Architecture (7-10 days)
- Next.js 14 + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS setup
- PostgreSQL database with R data migration
- Basic 2D visualization with D3.js
- Tennis-themed design system foundation

### **Sprint 2** - Interactive Data Exploration (7-10 days)
- 3D interactive racquet space with Three.js
- Advanced filtering system with real-time updates
- Detailed racquet profile pages
- Mobile-optimized touch controls

### **Sprint 3** - Intelligence & Comparison Features (7-10 days)
- AI-powered recommendation engine
- Side-by-side racquet comparison tools
- Playing style assessment and matching
- Data-driven insights and correlations

### **Sprint 4** - Polish & Production (7-10 days)
- Performance optimization (sub-3s load times)
- WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility compliance
- Production deployment and monitoring
- SEO optimization and social sharing

## 🎯 Key Features of This Plan

**Quality Assurance**: After each sprint, comprehensive code audits using Gemini Pro (architecture/performance) and O3 (logic/optimization) with mandatory feedback integration.

**Modern Tech Stack**: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Three.js for 3D visualization, PostgreSQL, and deployment on Vercel/Railway.

**User-Centric Design**: Beautiful tennis-themed interface that transforms complex racquet data into intuitive, actionable insights for tennis players.

**Progressive Complexity**: Each sprint builds on the previous, from basic functionality to sophisticated AI-powered recommendations.

The plan transforms your rich R-based tennis racquet analysis into a production-ready web application that tennis players will love using to discover their perfect racquet! 🎾

Initializes a TypeScript project and creates a tsconfig.json file.

Compile the project given the path to its configuration file, or to a folder with a 'tsconfig.json'.

--build, -b
Build one or more projects and their dependencies, if out of date

--showConfig
Print the final configuration instead of building.

type: boolean

Generate .d.ts files from TypeScript and JavaScript files in your project.

Only output d.ts files and not JavaScript files.
type: boolean
default: false

Create source map files for emitted JavaScript files.
type: boolean
default: false

Disable emitting files from a compilation.
type: boolean
default: false

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The conversation is summarized below:
Analysis:
Let me chronologically analyze the conversation to capture all the technical details and user requests:

1. **Initial User Request**: The user asked me to review the entire codebase (everything, including subfolders and csv files) and tell them what I found. This was an R-based tennis racquet analysis project.

2. **Codebase Analysis**: I discovered a comprehensive R-based system for scraping and analyzing tennis racquet reviews from Tennis Warehouse, including:
   - Web scraping infrastructure (main.R, discover_racquets.R, scrape_reviews.R)
   - Data processing and statistical analysis
   - Shiny applications for visualization
   - CSV data with 104 racquet models

3. **User's Vision**: The user then explained they wanted to transform this into "a clean and elegant web app" that would be "a way for people to explore tennis racquets in this space, to understand how verbal labels relate to physical characteristics of a racquet. it should be beautiful and clean rather than a shiny app. It should be a proper web app, using modern technologies."

4. **My Design Proposal**: I presented a comprehensive modern web app design including:
   - Interactive 3D racquet space visualization
   - Smart recommendation engine
   - Comparison tools
   - Modern tech stack (Next.js, TypeScript, Three.js, etc.)

5. **Sprint Planning Request**: The user asked to "devivide the plain into 4 markdown sprints labeled sprint1.md, sprint2.md, sprint3.md, sprint4.md ; by sprint 4 the app is complete, working, beautiful. after each sprint do a code audi using gemini pro and o3; take the feeedback, address major converns, then proceed; put this methodology and other sensible procedures in CLAUDE.md"

6. **Sprint Implementation**: I created:
   - CLAUDE.md: Development methodology with AI code review process
   - sprint1.md: Foundation & Core Architecture
   - sprint2.md: Interactive Data Exploration  
   - sprint3.md: Intelligence & Comparison Features
   - sprint4.md: Polish & Production

7. **Sprint 1 Execution**: User said "start sprint 1" and I began implementing:
   - Next.js 14 project setup with TypeScript
   - Tennis-themed design system with Tailwind CSS
   - Data processing utilities for R data migration
   - Database schema design

Key technical decisions made:
- Used React 18 instead of 19 due to Three.js compatibility issues
- Implemented Tailwind 4 with inline theme system
- Created comprehensive TypeScript interfaces for racquet data
- Built data processing pipeline to convert R data to JSON format

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user wanted to transform an existing R-based tennis racquet analysis system into a modern, elegant web application. The goal is to create a beautiful, clean web app using modern technologies that helps people explore tennis racquets and understand how verbal performance labels relate to physical characteristics. They specifically requested a 4-sprint development plan with AI code reviews after each sprint, and wanted to start with Sprint 1 implementation.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Next.js 14 with App Router and TypeScript
   - Tailwind CSS 4 with inline theme system
   - Tennis-themed design system (court green colors, tennis ball yellow accents)
   - Data migration from R analysis to JSON/PostgreSQL
   - 3D visualization with Three.js and D3.js
   - AI-powered recommendation engine
   - Sprint-based development with Gemini Pro and O3 code reviews
   - Performance optimization and accessibility compliance
   - Modern web standards (WCAG 2.1 AA, Core Web Vitals)

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **CLAUDE.md**: Development methodology document defining sprint-based approach with AI code reviews, quality standards, and technical architecture principles
   
   - **sprint1.md**: Foundation & Core Architecture sprint plan covering Next.js setup, database design, API development, and basic 2D visualization
   
   - **sprint2.md**: Interactive Data Exploration sprint for 3D visualization, advanced filtering, and mobile optimization
   
   - **sprint3.md**: Intelligence & Comparison Features sprint for recommendation engine and smart features
   
   - **sprint4.md**: Polish & Production sprint for performance optimization and production deployment
   
   - **tennis-racquet-explorer/package.json**: Project dependencies including React 18, Next.js 15, D3.js, Three.js, Framer Motion, Zustand, and development tools
   
   - **tennis-racquet-explorer/src/app/globals.css**: Complete tennis-themed design system with CSS custom properties:
   ```css
   :root {
     /* Tennis Court Colors */
     --court-green: #228B22;
     --court-green-light: #32CD32;
     --court-green-dark: #006400;
     --tennis-ball: #CCFF00;
     --tennis-ball-dark: #9ACD32;
   }
   ```
   
   - **tennis-racquet-explorer/src/app/layout.tsx**: Root layout with Inter font, comprehensive metadata for SEO, and tennis-themed configuration
   
   - **tennis-racquet-explorer/src/app/page.tsx**: Landing page with tennis-themed hero section, feature grid, and development progress tracking
   
   - **tennis-racquet-explorer/src/lib/types.ts**: Comprehensive TypeScript interfaces for Racquet, Review, Brand, and visualization types with all performance categories
   
   - **tennis-racquet-explorer/src/lib/data-processor.ts**: Data processing utility class for converting R data format to structured TypeScript objects, including spec parsing and performance averaging
   
   - **tennis-racquet-explorer/scripts/process-data.ts**: Script to parse the R data file and generate JSON files for the application

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **React version compatibility error**: Initial setup used React 19 which conflicted with Three.js libraries. Fixed by downgrading to React 18.3.1 and corresponding TypeScript types.
   - **Package installation conflicts**: Used `npm install --legacy-peer-deps` to resolve peer dependency conflicts between React versions and visualization libraries.
   - **TypeScript configuration**: Had to navigate to correct project directory to run TypeScript compilation checks.
   - **Development server port conflict**: Port 3000 was in use, server automatically used port 3001.

5. Problem Solving:
   Successfully set up a modern Next.js 14 project with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS 4. Resolved React version compatibility issues with Three.js libraries. Created a comprehensive data processing pipeline to migrate from R analysis format to structured web application data. Established tennis-themed design system with proper color palette and typography.

6. All user messages:
   - "review the codebase; everything, including subfolders and csv files; then tell me what you find."
   - "ok, this was a demo. We'd like to make this a clean and elegant web app. It would be a way for people to explore tennis racquets in this sace, to understand how verbal labels relate to physical characteristics of a racquet. it should be beautiful and clean rather than a shiny app. It should be a proper web app, using modern technologies. It should be modern and elegant in appearance, though simple. can you design something?"
   - "can we devivide the plain into 4 markdown sprints labeled sprint1.md, sprint2.md, sprint3.md, sprint4.md ; by sprint 4 the app is complete, working, beautiful. after each sprint do a code audi using gemini pro and o3; take the feeedback, address major converns, then proceed; put this methodology and other sensible procedures in CLAUDE.md"
   - "start sprint 1"

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Complete Sprint 1 Task 2: Run the data processing script to convert R data to JSON
   - Sprint 1 Task 3: Build REST API endpoints for racquet data
   - Sprint 1 Task 4: Create reusable UI components with tennis theme
   - Sprint 1 Task 5: Implement basic 2D scatter plot visualization with D3.js
   - Sprint 1 Task 6: Code audit with Gemini Pro and O3

8. Current Work:
   I was in the middle of Sprint 1 Task 2 (Database Design & Data Migration). I had just completed creating the data processing infrastructure including TypeScript types, data processor class, and processing script. The current state shows I was setting up the foundation for converting the R analysis data into a structured format for the web application. I had successfully created:
   - Complete type definitions for the application data structures
   - A DataProcessor class that can parse R data and extract racquets, reviews, and performance averages
   - A processing script ready to convert the actual data file

9. Optional Next Step:
   Continue with Sprint 1 Task 2 by running the data processing script to convert the R data file (`all_review_data.txt`) into JSON format for the web application. The user specifically asked to "start sprint 1" and I was working on the database design and data migration portion, which is the next logical step to complete before moving to API development.

Direct quote from recent work: "Now let's create a script to process the R data and generate JSON files" - I had just completed creating the processing script and the next step would be to execute it to generate the actual data files needed for the application..

content.text:
Perfect! Sprint 1 API Development is complete. I successfully created a comprehensive REST API with:

- `/api/racquets` - List racquets with filtering and pagination
- `/api/racquets/[id]` - Get individual racquet with reviews
- `/api/brands` - List all brands
- `/api/stats` - Get data statistics

The API successfully serves data for 127 racquets, 8334 reviews, and 12 brands with full filtering capabilities. The data migration from R format to structured JSON is complete and working perfectly.

Now proceeding to Sprint 1 Task 4 - UI Components with tennis-themed design system.
